resume:
ANIKA SØRENSEN
Product Designer (Junior) — UX / UI
Berlin, Germany | anika.sorensen@example.com | portfolio: anika.design

PROFILE
Junior product designer with about two and a half years of hands-on experience designing for web and mobile across two early-stage companies. I came into design from a front-end background, which means I am comfortable working closely with engineers, talking in terms of components and constraints, and prototyping interactions that are actually buildable. I care most about the boring, unglamorous parts of design that move numbers: clear information hierarchy, reducing the number of steps in a flow, and writing microcopy that removes confusion. I like to validate decisions with real usability evidence rather than taste alone, and I try to keep a tight feedback loop with research, product, and engineering. I am looking to grow under stronger design mentorship and to take on more end-to-end ownership of complex flows.

EXPERIENCE

Product Designer — Treva (B2C personal-finance app), 2023-present
Treva is a Series A consumer-finance startup helping young people budget and save. I am one of three designers, and I report to a design lead. I own the onboarding and account-linking surfaces and contribute to the broader design system.
- Redesigned the bank-account linking flow, which was the single biggest drop-off point in onboarding. I ran the usability sessions myself, reworked the flow from five screens to three, rewrote the consent and error copy, and partnered with two engineers through implementation. Activation through the linking step improved substantially after launch.
- Built and maintained the form-input and validation components in the design system, including error, focus, and disabled states, and documented usage guidelines so engineers could self-serve.
- Introduced a lightweight weekly usability-testing habit on the team: five unmoderated tests per week through a remote testing tool, which gave us a steady trickle of evidence instead of big-bang research.
- Collaborated with a content designer to standardize error messaging across the app, replacing vague system errors with specific, recovery-oriented copy.
- Partnered with the analytics team to instrument the onboarding funnel so design changes could be measured against activation rather than guessed at.

UX/UI Designer (part-time, then full-time) — Kettle (B2B scheduling tool), 2021-2023
Kettle was a small scheduling-software startup. I started part-time while finishing my studies and moved to full-time.
- Designed the calendar and availability views for the core scheduling product, balancing density of information against clarity for first-time users.
- Created the company's first reusable component library in the design tool, which cut design hand-off ambiguity and sped up engineering implementation.
- Ran a redesign of the meeting-invite flow informed by support tickets and session recordings, reducing a class of user confusion that had been generating repeat complaints.
- Worked directly with two founders and three engineers in a fast, low-process environment where I had to make and defend decisions quickly.

FRONT-END BACKGROUND
Before committing to design full-time I spent about a year doing front-end work, building responsive marketing pages and small internal tools. This is why I am fluent in HTML, CSS, and component-based thinking, and why I prototype in code when a clickable prototype cannot capture an interaction. I see this as a core part of my design identity rather than a separate skill.

EDUCATION
B.A., Interaction Design — University of the Arts (2022). Coursework in human-centered design, visual design fundamentals, and design research methods. Thesis project on reducing cognitive load in financial dashboards.

SKILLS
Design: interaction design, information architecture, usability testing, design systems, prototyping, UX writing fundamentals, accessibility basics (WCAG AA awareness). Tools: Figma (advanced), FigJam, Maze, Dovetail, basic HTML/CSS/JavaScript, Storybook familiarity. Ways of working: close engineering collaboration, funnel instrumentation with analytics partners, lightweight continuous usability testing.

SELECTED PROJECTS
- Treva account-linking redesign: cut a five-screen flow to three, reworked consent and error states, and tied the change to an activation metric in the onboarding funnel.
- Treva design-system form components: shipped documented, stateful input and validation components used across the app.
- Kettle meeting-invite redesign: used support tickets and session recordings to diagnose confusion and redesign the flow.
- Kettle component library: created the first shared library, improving design-to-engineering hand-off.

WHAT I AM LOOKING FOR
I want to join a team with strong, generous design mentorship where I can deepen my craft and take ownership of harder, more ambiguous problems. I am drawn to products where small interaction and copy details have a measurable impact on whether people succeed at a task. I value teams that instrument their work, that test with real users regularly, and that treat engineering as a design partner rather than a downstream implementer. I am comfortable being the person who sweats the empty states, the error messages, and the edge cases, because I believe that is where a lot of real product quality lives. Concretely, the kind of work that energizes me is taking a flow that quietly loses people, instrumenting it so the drop-off is visible, sitting with five or six real users until the friction is obvious, and then shipping a tighter version and watching the number move. I also enjoy the design-system side of the craft: building components that are accessible by default, documenting their states so engineers never have to guess, and treating consistency as a feature rather than an afterthought. Over the next few years I want to develop sharper judgment about when to follow a convention and when to break it, and to get better at framing ambiguous problems before any pixels exist. I would rather work somewhere that holds design to a measurable bar than somewhere that rewards polish for its own sake, and I am happy to be the most junior person in the room as long as I am learning from people whose craft is clearly ahead of mine.
jd: Product Designer (Junior) — Haldenmark, a consumer mobile app. We are hiring an early-career product designer to own onboarding and activation flows end to end. You will run usability testing, design and prototype in Figma, contribute to our design system, and partner tightly with product, engineering, and analytics. We expect you to ground design decisions in evidence and to be able to walk through a project as a complete story: the problem and its impact, the goal you set, the specific design decisions you made and why, and the measured outcome. Junior is fine; clarity of thinking and honesty about results are what we screen for.

history.q: Tell me about your role at Treva — what surfaces do you own and who do you work with?
You mention the account-linking flow was the biggest drop-off. How did you know that?
What was the activation rate through that linking step before you touched it?
What goal did you set for the redesign?
Walk me through the usability sessions — how many users, what did you learn?
Why did you decide to cut it from five screens to three specifically? What got merged or removed?
Talk me through the consent and error copy changes — give me a concrete before-and-after.
What was your role versus the two engineers' during implementation?
After launch, what happened to the activation rate, and over what period?
How did you isolate your redesign from other changes happening at the same time?
Was there a tradeoff you had to make in that redesign that you weren't fully happy with?
If you ran it again, what would you do differently?
Give me the whole story in one go: situation, your goal, what you did, and the measured result.

candidate_last_answer: Sure. Situation: at Treva, bank-account linking was the single biggest drop-off in onboarding — only about 48% of users who reached the linking screen actually completed it, and the funnel analytics plus five usability sessions showed people bailed at a five-screen sequence with a scary, generic consent screen. Task: my lead and I set a target to get completion above 60% within the quarter without loosening any of the security or consent requirements. Action: I personally ran six unmoderated usability tests, then redesigned the flow from five screens to three — I merged the bank-search and credentials steps and removed a redundant confirmation screen, rewrote the consent copy from a wall of legal text into a plain-language summary with the legal detail behind a disclosure, and replaced the generic 『Something went wrong』 error with specific recovery copy like 『We couldn't reach your bank — try again in a moment』. I paired with two engineers through build and we shipped behind a feature flag. Result: over the four weeks after launch, completion through the linking step went from 48% to 63%, which cleared our target, and support tickets tagged 『linking error』 dropped by about 35%. I isolated it with a 50/50 flag rollout, so the lift was measured against a concurrent control rather than just pre/post. The one tradeoff I wasn't thrilled about: to hit the deadline we shipped the plain-language consent in English only and the localized versions lagged by two weeks, so non-English users saw the old screen a little longer than I wanted.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: completion through the linking step went from 48% to 63%

expected-yield: durability and rigor behind the headline metric — whether the 48% to 63% lift held beyond the four-week window, the sample size and significance of the 50/50 flag test, and whether the localization lag dented the gain for non-English cohorts
